data.title: CBRE: Beat And Raise Quarter Justifies A 'Buy'
data.description:
I'm staying bullish on CBRE Group, Inc. (NYSE:CBRE). There are favorable read-throughs from its results/guidance pertaining to the emergence of new growth engines.

Its better-than-anticipated 3Q24 showing was the subject of my earlier October 25, 2024, write-up.

Q3 Earnings Were A Positive Surprise

CBRE's latest 10-Q filing was published on Thursday, Oct. 23.

The firm saw its normalized bottom line surge +34% year-on-year to $1.61/share in Jul-Sep '25. This was 10% ahead of the consensus projection of $1.46, as per S&P Capital IQ.

The "Advisory Services/AS" and "Project Management/PM" divisions were the stars for CBRE.

AS's YoY top-line expansion improved from 2Q2025's 14% to 3Q25's 17%. The "APAC" or "Asia-Pacific" Leasing sub-segment recorded a "revenue growth of 22% (23% local currency), led by India and Japan" according to the results release.

PM's 3Q2025 turnover was 20% higher vis-a-vis that for the same quarter a year ago. The unit achieved an inferior +14% growth in the Apr-Jun period this year. In its earnings slides, CBRE revealed that "from a client perspective, (Project Management) demand was driven by data center work for hyperscalers."

Also, the group's EBITDA-to-sales metric widened by +40 bps (basis points) to 8.0% in the recent three-month period on YoY terms. This represented a 0.3 ppts (percentage points) beat.

My late-Oct '24 write-up highlighted "the high fixed-cost nature of CBRE's real estate services business operations" implied substantial "operating leverage effects." It's understandable that the enterprise's profitability was enhanced by revenue increases for AS/PM.

The strong financial momentum for CBRE is likely to be sustained going forward, as I will detail in the next section.

Company Has Become More Optimistic About Full-Year Prospects

CBRE revised its FY25 EPS guide from $6.15 to $6.30, implying a 24% jump compared to the prior fiscal year. That also came in above the sell-side's estimate of $6.21.

My opinion is that management's forecasts are realistic.

The main factors that supported CBRE's 3Q25 outperformance are intact for the foreseeable future. One of them is "Data Centers" or "DCs." Its DC-related revenue went up by two-fifths YoY to $0.7 billion for 3Q2025.

The scale of CBRE's DC-focused operations is still modest, which suggests huge potential.

The DC business's top line and EBITDA accounted for 7% and one-tenth of group-level numbers, respectively, in this quarter.

CBRE noted at the analyst briefing that its subsidiary "Turner & Townsend does a lot of Project Management work, cost consultancy for data centers." It also mentioned, "we have a very large data center brokerage business in our advisory business." I think that the outlook for PM/AS is bright.

Its results call commentary drew attention to "$900 million of embedded profits" pertaining to the "monetization" of "large data center development sites" in "later this year (late-2025) or next year." I anticipate that the group's near-term earnings will be boosted by such gains.

Separately, CBRE is in the early innings of geographical diversification. It derived three-tenths of FY24 sales from operations located outside the U.S./U.K. Asia is where CBRE could possibly broaden its presence in a big way.

In the recent quarter, Japan and India as a whole contributed $0.4 billion of revenues for the company, or equivalent of a 30+% increase. At its 3Q call, it stressed that "we have large businesses in both countries (India/Japan)" that are "positioned for sustained secular growth."

Tailwinds For Asian Property Markets

CBRE's own research has identified a number of "cyclical" and "structural" drivers relating to Asia as presented above. It makes sense for the firm to raise its exposure there.

I'm confident that the company will continue to do well, given the attractiveness of the APAC and DC end markets.

Eyes On Capital Allocation

I find it intriguing that CBRE paused buybacks in the third quarter of this year. During the same period, it concluded acquisition deals worth about $30 million.

Management reiterated at the Q3 meeting that "our capital allocation priorities remain" unchanged. Its approach is to "prioritize M&A (Mergers & Acquisitions) and co-investment into REI (Real Estate Investment)" and "use the remainder of the free cash flow that we generate for share repurchases."

In my view, CBRE is leaning towards utilizing capital for inorganic growth (as opposed to cash distributions), judging by its actions and remarks. This should boost its inorganic prospects.

The group has no issues funding any potential transactions. Its net debt-to-EBITDA was a comfortable 1.2 times as of the end of 3Q25.

You Should Consider These Bear-Case Scenarios

CBRE may suffer from poor results in the quarters ahead, if demand for Asian real estate and data centers weakens.

There is the fear that it might pay a hefty premium to take over low-quality assets or businesses.

My Closing Remarks

I've left CBRE's existing "Buy" rating unchanged.

In the Oct-2024 update, I emphasized that the stock can even "command a P/E multiple closer to its peak valuation", as it is expected to "exceed its historical peak EPS in FY 2025."

My argument is still valid. Its updated FY2025 bottom-line guidance of $6.30/share is 9% better than the previous historical record ($5.80/share) set four years ago. The firm is well-positioned to meet its target, thanks to the DC- and APAC- related opportunities.

CBRE's trailing earnings multiple reached a level as high as 50x in the past five years, or a mean of 28.9x.

It is trading at 22.5x FY26 P/E now. Mean reversion translates into an upside of +28% (28.9/22.5).

data.title: Stride: Strong Prospects, But Valuation Keeps Me Neutral
data.description:
Thesis

I'm rating Stride (NYSE:LRN) as a Hold. While a rebound is likely given the consolidation in prices since September 16, the valuation, both relative and absolute, stops me from being a convinced bull. Plus, the CEO and CFO have been selling. Lastly, there are some active lawsuits against the company, which in part fueled the current 10% short interest.

The pandemic changed education forever, especially with the surge of online and hybrid schooling. Stride's value has multiplied sevenfold since then. The last 5 quarters have been impressive, accelerating revenue growth. As a result, Wall Street revisions are massively upward, and as of October 19, the company is hitting a +A in the revisions metric of Seeking Alpha's quant section.

The prospects, I believe, are modest but good; however, when factoring growth into a valuation model, they suggest the company is fairly valued.

Company Overview

Stride is an educational technology company that focuses mainly on general education (K-12) and career readiness programs, better known as career and technical education (CTE).

The table below shows the revenue segments. General education revenues have slightly dropped from 62% to 60% of total revenue, while career learning programs grew from 38% to 40%. Regarding growth, the clear star segment is the career readiness programs for middle and high school, which grew 35% Y/Y last fiscal year. Keep in mind the company's fiscal year closes on June 30, aligning with the school year.

It's interesting to note that revenue is not just growing—it's accelerating, jumping from 11% to 18% year-over-year in the last fiscal year.

The company has beaten its prior growth rate for five consecutive quarters. This acceleration is fueled by several factors, mainly: the post-pandemic legacy of hybrid school, the surge in AI LLMs, and the increasing support for the School Choice movement. Also, the company appears to be gaining market share, as its growth is outpacing the industry average. This suggests a competitive advantage in the services they provide.

In terms of guidance, the company expects between 10% and 15% enrollment growth Y/Y in the next quarter. These figures are based on 'stronger-than-last-year' application volumes, according to the CEO. In other words, the next quarter could bring happy surprises for bulls of this company. However, at the same time, analysts are expecting a 21% quarterly growth Y/Y in 1Q'26; so, those growth rates may already be priced in.

Industry and the School Choice Movement

According to Grandview Research, the global Education Technology (EdTech) industry is expected to grow at a 13.3% CAGR between 2025 and 2030. Meanwhile, Cognitive Market Research forecasts a 15.5% global CAGR and 15% for the US until 2033. These industry growth rates are very appealing when considering Stride. It's easier for Stride to grow when the entire industry is growing, as there is less need to fight for market share.

Strictly speaking, the company operates in two similar but distinct subgroups of the EdTech industry. Regarding the K-12 education market, Mordor Intelligence expects it to grow 10.15% until 2030 (this is lower than the overall edutech expected CAGR). The main drivers that they cite are the following. Digital device and broadband penetration, mainly in rural areas. AI-powered learning analytics that enhance student outcomes, and a rising demand for personalized and adaptive learning platforms. Also, it's worth noting that while they describe the market as highly competitive, it's also highly fragmented as of now. This last point is a two-sided issue. The pros for Stride are faster growth, more opportunities for specialization and differentiation, and lower overall competition. Conversely, the cons include high price competition (especially for undifferentiated products) and potential inefficiencies because of a lack of industry standards (let alone the education-regulated aspects). However, I believe that the positive sides outweigh the negative ones.

The other segment, which focuses on career learning, is a more complex one. On one side, as we all know, the AI surge is changing fast the jobs and professions landscape. However, the company appears to be not only avoiding harm from the AI surge but actually is benefiting from it. The middle-high school career learning segment is growing at 35% Y/Y, in the same 12 months that the nation and the whole world have been impacted by a surge of ChatGPT and a wide range of LLMs. This is not a minor aspect, and that's one of the reasons that I'm being highly optimistic with this company (despite the current valuation that dissuades me a bit).

In recent years, school choice and K-12 Hybrid Schooling have been growing in demand.

Polling data reveals that 49% of parents would prefer their child to learn from home at least one day a week. While 10% want full-time homeschooling, the remaining 39% of parents desire their child to learn at home one to four days a week, with the remaining days attending school on campus. Another parent poll released this month indicates that an astonishing 64% of parents indicated that if they were looking for a new school for their child, they would enroll him or her in a hybrid school.

These percentages in the picture below are quite impressive for even online schooling at 47%. Hybrid and Microschool (the latter is some sort of group home-schooling) are indicating a clear shift in what parents are looking for.

Valuation

Regarding valuation, when compared to its historical average, it heavily depends on what metric we are considering. For example, EV to revenues and price to book will suggest that the company is expensive on a historical basis. EV to revenues ratio is 105% above its historical mean, and the price to book is 94% above.

However, these metrics, if we consider them in isolation, won't be representative because they fail to account for the growth in earnings in the last quarters, nor even consider the acceleration in revenue growth.

When we consider relative valuation ratios compared to the overall sector, all of them indicate an average overvaluation of 19%, or a downside of 16%. However, the key and most representative ratios for this company are the forward P/E metrics. That's because of the impressive change and acceleration in growth in earnings in the last quarters, coupled with the highly likely continuation of this kind of growth.

It's also important to mention the sales-based ratios, which indicate the most overvaluation relative to the sector, but this is also directly attributable to the acceleration in sales that I already discussed previously.

So, to get a better read on this company, we need to make forward growth estimates and check both a reverse valuation and an absolute valuation based on what I believe are reasonable assumptions.

My main assumptions are these: earnings growth starts at 15% in the first year and then declines annually to 4.5%. This implies a CAGR of 9.7% for the next 10 years. While this is optimistic, I find it reasonable given the acceleration in revenues that we noted in each of the last five quarters. The cost of equity might be below average for a US company based on the beta calculation. However, most sites are calculating beta below 0.5x, which I think is not representative of risk. So, I decided to use something more average.

How could I be wrong? There are two scenarios: earnings growing significantly below 9.7% or significantly above that. So, why am I not buying this stock? I believe it won't be easy to grow at a CAGR above 10% over the next 10 years. Can Stride achieve that? Yes, I think it can, but we should ask ourselves, How likely is it? There is uncertainty related to AI and regulations, not to mention competition. However, I'm somewhat optimistic regarding revenues and earnings for this company, expecting modest growth rates, but not double-digit ones.

Risks

While there are several risks, the main three to consider are: regulatory and political, competition, and legal risk.

Competition is surging on multiple fronts. Public schools are launching hybrid programs, and companies such as Pearson, McGraw-Hill, and Cengage Group, which are direct competitors, are offering K-12 education programs. Also, there are indirect competitors such as the MOOCs (Massive Open Online Courses), such as Coursera and Udemy. It's worth noting that the AI surge increases the uncertainty for almost all companies' prospects, especially for EdTech ones. In other words, creating new educational programs will become easier over time. This means that from both the technological and marketing point of view, Stride will have to develop and offer a Unique Value Proposition.

The second risk is political and regulatory; in other words, state funding and regulations are critical for Stride. Political shifts, both partisan and intra-partisan, federal and state-related, can affect the company. However, the 2025 polls indicate that 57% of Democrats support school choice, while 77% of Republicans support it. So, potential adverse political shifts are less likely to happen.

Lastly, there are several active lawsuits against Stride. The main one is the Gallup-McKinley County Schools (GMCS) case. The high uncertainty surrounding it prevents us from estimating both the probability of an adverse ruling and the magnitude of the potential loss. In other words, if there is an adverse ruling, we don't know if the total cost for Stride might be 1% or 10% of sales. Only time will tell; however, we should keep it in mind, regardless of our view on the company, but especially for those who are bullish.

As of today, October 23, the company has a short interest of 10.2%. As a rule of thumb, levels up to 4% are considered "normal", but when it reaches 10%, it starts to be an issue to be analyzed deeper. More recently, since 2021, the average was 10.11, reaching its peak a year ago in October 2024 at 17%.

It is also worth noting that both the CFO and CEO have been selling shares recently. The average selling price was $152 and $149, respectively. The total amounts sold since August 2025 are $3.6MM for the CFO and $17.4MM for the CEO and Chair of the Board. Please, don't get me wrong, this does not imply that something is wrong with the company. My thesis is a 'hold' one and not a 'sell' one. Valuation, combined with growth, becomes an important factor here. But when we see this kind of insider selling, we should be more cautious about buying.

Conclusion

Wrapping up, it's very interesting to highlight how Stride is revolutionizing education. Also, I believe that despite competition, the AI surge, coupled with the great uncertainty that comes with it, and regulatory risks, this company holds good prospects and can achieve upper single-digit growth in the next decade.

However, factoring this kind of growth into a valuation model still doesn't justify buying the stock at its current valuation. Furthermore, relative valuation suggests a mild overvaluation. Finally, all the warnings and caveats mentioned in the risk section solidified my decision to hold off on buying this company at the current valuation.

data.title: FDVV: A Rising Star Or Risky Bet For Dividend Growth Investors?
data.description:
It is easier than ever to invest in a diversified basket of stocks, but there's a record number of ETFs to choose from. In fact, there's now more ETFs than individual stocks on the market, over 4,200. Keeping a simple portfolio is anything but simple. One of the challenging double-edged swords I wield as both an investor and analyst is constantly re-evaluating my own portfolio to see what new ETF methodologies are on the market.

The core of my dividend growth portfolio is simply Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO). I defend this combination frequently in my research, and it tends to be my reference point based on performance, dividend metrics, and diversification. Lately, social media has really hyped up the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:FDVV) as a potential competitor and complement to a dividend growth portfolio

Performance & Methodology

Over the last five years, FDVV has quietly outperformed both SCHD and DGRO, delivering 129% total returns compared to SCHD's 63% and DGRO's 84% total return. However, since FDVV's inception in September, 2016, it's kept pace with DGRO, but is still outperforming SCHD by 45% over its 9 year span. One thing to caution investors of is this pullaway didn't occur until after April 2025 this year: that's a huge gain in a very short amount of time.

In any case, FDVV is clearly tracking in the right direction on total returns. An understanding of the fund's holdings and methodology will help us understand what alpha this fund potentially brings to a dividend investor's portfolio.

Holdings and Methodology

With $7.21B AUM, FDVV is significantly smaller than SCHD's $70B and DGRO's $34.6B AUM, but it's nothing to balk at and is still highly liquid. The fund has a higher expense ratio of 0.16%, twice that of DGRO and SCHD's 0.08% and 0.06%, respectively, still not a huge difference in absolute dollars but not a good selling point. FDVV has a high concentration in technology and financials at 26.5% and 18.8%, with notable companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), and Visa Class A (V) in the top 10 ranks.

Based on the Fidelity Factor and Income Indices Methodology, the objective of the Fidelity High Dividend Index is to select stocks of large and mid-capitalization dividend-paying companies that are expected to continue to pay and grow their dividends. The intended alpha is to provide higher relative dividend yield with sector tilts. The fund allocates at least 90% of its holdings to the largest 1,000 US stocks based on market cap and similarly up to 10% to developed international stocks, with 40% to the highest yielding sectors.

A composite score is then calculated by selecting the top stocks in each sector based on dividend yield, payout ratio, and dividend growth at 70%, 15%, and 15%, respectively. Securities lacking a current or indicated dividend yield or in the top 5% of payout ratios are excluded, and securities are selected by sector: top decile for sectors with over 100 securities, top quintile for 25-100 securities, and all for sectors with fewer than 25. The fund is reconstituted and rebalanced annually, but dividend payments are monitored regularly for cuts and pauses.

Dividend Growth

A few things I like about FDVV's methodology are that international stocks can make up a minority share of the fund, unlike SCHD or DGRO. I like the dividend yield focus and tilt toward sectors as well. A few things I don't care for with this fund's methodology are that dividend growth is only 15% of the weighting. Additionally, for a such a high yield focused ETF, the dividend yield of FDVV is only 3.10%, putting it in the middle between DGRO's 2.04% and SCHD's 3.86% yield. This is more so a reflection of the low-yield market these days.

Dividend Metrics FDVV SCHD DGRO
Dividend Yield ('TTM') 3.10% 3.86% 2.04%
5-Year Dividend Growth Rate 10.42% 10.38% 6.87%
Dividend Growth CAGR to 2024 -7.11% 12.23% 8.85%
Years of Uninterrupted Dividend Growth 0 13 10
Yield on Cost (YOC) Since Inception 6.88% 12.13% 5.58%

While the 5-year dividend growth rate is great at 10.42%, the CAGR is -7.11%, with annual payout growth all over the place. Compared to SCHD and DGRO, which both have completely uninterrupted dividend growth averaging around 10.5% CAGR. FDVV's methodology to generate consistent dividend growth is lacking and is my main concern with dividend ETFs competing with SCHD and DGRO.

Diversification

FDVV overlap with SCHD is only 16% by weight, with a much heavier tilt toward technology, financials, utilities, and Real Estate and much less in Health Care, Energy, and Industrials. For a pair of funds with only around 100 holdings, a 16% overlap and meaningful sector tilts offer meaningful diversification here.

FDVV's overlap with DGRO, on the other hand, is 38% by weight, with a surprisingly heavy tilt in Real Estate and even more pronounced technology allocation. I don't thing these two mix all that well together based on the lack of diversification you would get, but it is interesting that both funds have competed on total returns since inception despite DGRO having more conservative weighting and not including NVDA. The yield of FDVV/DGOR would be more like 2.5% with less dividend growth compared to an FDVV/SCHD combination at 3.5% with at least higher dividend growth.

Risk Analysis

With 46% of the fund's holdings in just two sectors, probably a theme every year based on the methodology, FDVV carries sector concentration risk. In my recent publication on SCHD I observed that much of the fund's underperformance year to date was driven by its own concentration, which is less common, in just two sectors: Consumer Staples and Energy. The focus on higher yield is sometimes a response to undervalued companies and sectors, but it's still a bet those valuations will correct higher in the near future. FDVV's 15% weighting on dividend growth risks continued inconsistent dividend increases if market yields compress further. This makes the fund less competitive for investors focused on both yield and dividend growth, like SCHD and DGRO's more balanced methodologies, which prioritize sustained dividend growth.

Looking Ahead

If the AI and Tech sector momentum sustains, FDVV could continue being a top performer. With demand for data centers and semiconductors forecasted to increase substantially, dividend payers like NVDA and AVGO stand to deliver both capital appreciation and dividend growth to the fund. As the rate-cutting cycle continues well into 2026, FDVV's higher yield and 5-year dividend growth stand to attract investors rotating toward income. After all, there's nearly $7.5T in money market funds on the sidelines waiting to be deployed into assets as rates come down.

Overall, FDVV has performed well the last 5 years and is a solid dividend ETF to consider in a dividend investor's portfolio, but its dividend growth is not consistent enough for me to invest in it at this time. If its dividend growth metrics stabilize, I wouldn't mind pairing it with SCHD to balance out sector tilts, add some international exposure, and increase my yield compared to my DGRO pairing. FDVV's main issue at this point is the yield is middle of the road and dividend growth is not consistent, so there's no clear alpha for my portfolio unless both metrics can stand up against DGRO. I'll definitely continue to track FDVV's performance over the next few years before reconsidering.

data.title: AbraSilver: The Silver-Gold Developer Moving From Discovery To Execution
data.description:
Strong Re-Rating Since August

When I posted my previous analysis about AbraSilver Resource Corp. (OTCQX:ABBRF) in August, the share price rounded to $4.05, and the Diablillos project was still perceived by many as an early promise. Nowadays, just two months later, the stock rose 1.7%, underperforming the S&P 500 performance (+5.6%), mainly because of the recent 8% drop following the pullback in silver prices. Still, that doesn't change the underlying story: the company delivered execution, financing, and solid technical results that reconfigured the market's perception.

Throughout this period, ABBRF strengthened its balance sheet, expanded its leadership team with international weight figures, and confirmed the resource expansion in key zones. Sometimes, the value doesn't change for one or more ounces, but for the trustworthiness that such consistency generates.

Moreover, the market's environment plays in favour. With silver moving within the range of $50-55/oz, its highest level in more than a decade, and gold remaining above $4.000, the appeal of low-cost projects and high operating leverage like Diablillos has multiplied. In my opinion, that context, combined with the technical and financial capacity that the company has demonstrated, justifies keeping a positive position.

From Discovery to Development

I have been following the Diablillos project, in Salta, Argentina, for a while now. What started as a promising exploration turned into an asset of global scale. In just five years, ABBRF duplicated its measured and indicated resources, reaching 350 Moz AgEq, conformed by 199 silver Moz and 1.7 gold Moz, with a discovery cost of just $0.11/oz AgEq.

The last drilling results confirmed the system's consistency and potential. In Oculto East, about 140 meters to 0.57 g/t of gold have been intercepted, including zones of 2.60 g/t Au, while 32 meters to 100 g/t of silver have been obtained in JAC, extending the mineralization beyond the pit's conceptual limit.

The CEO John Miniotis expressed the following:

These latest drill results highlight the strong continuity of wide gold zones at Oculto East, together with additional near-surface silver mineralization at JAC. The potential to expand both gold and silver resources well beyond the current Mineral Resource conceptual open pit further demonstrates the scale of Diablillos.

And I stop at the word "scale", given that I consider that it resumes what's essential. Diablillos is no longer an exploratory Project, but a story of development.

Towards the end of September, ABBR added a key figure: Marie Inkster, ex CEO of Lundin Mining (OTCPK:LUNMF) and current director of Cameco (CCJ) and Foran Mining (OTCQX:FMCXF), assumed the presidency of the board. Her addition is more than symbolic, because it marks a turning point as AbraSilver transitions institutionally into its execution phase.

Financial Strength and Strategic Backing

On October 6, ABBRF announced a "bought deal" of C$43.4MM, leaded by National Bank Financial and Beacon Securities, at a price of C$7.10/share, with participation of Kinross Gold (KGC) and Central Puerto (CEPU), companies that I have previously analyzed.

Miniotis, in words:

The funds raised will help accelerate the development of our high-quality Diablillos silver-gold project and further position AbraSilver as one of the leading silver-gold development companies in the Americas"

With more than C$42MM in cash and with no debt, ABBRF is ranked among the best-capitalized junior companies, and that solidity allows it to advance towards the DFS without resorting to dilution.

Currently, the company has a market capitalization of approximately C$1.07B, while its NPV5% (spot) reaches C$4.1B, which shows us the project's strong operative leverage.

In order to illustrate this point in more detail, ABBRF estimates that each 10% increase in the price of metals raises NPV approximately 30%. And it's that elasticity, that multiplies value without increasing debt, is one of the reasons why I still see ABBRF as undervalued.

Silver Market Context: A Metal Regaining Strategic Value

Silver is once again the protagonist, given in October that it traded between $50-55/oz, its highest level in more than ten years.

But this time, it's no longer about mere speculation, but about the energy transition having changed the rules of the game, given that the industrial demand - from solar panels to semiconductors - lead the silver consumption to record - breaking levels, right where the real rates are lower and coins are losing power. It's a combination that returns silver its strategical value.

In this context, ABBRF is benefited in a direct way. Just as I mentioned in the previous section, the project's economic model proves that each 10% increase in gold or silver prices raises NPV5% to around 30%. Thus, the current range of prices implies a project value of C$4.1B to C$4.8B, before the base range of C$3.5B of mid-year.

A Project of Global Scale

Just as I analysed in the previous article, the December 2024 PFS already set Diablillos as a worldwide competitive development, with a NPV5% (base case) of C$1.046B, an IRR of 28%, a two-year payback, and a $12.67/oz AgEq AISC, placing it in the first cost quartile.

However, the October 2025 corporate presentation took that story a step further, since, according to AbraSilver's estimates, at spot prices, the NPV5% rises to C$4.1B, with an IRR above 70% and a payback of less than one year.

The chief geologist, Dave O'Connor explained it in clarity:

Our drilling continues to demonstrate that gold and silver mineralization extend far beyond the limits of the current pit model. Diablillos has the potential to evolve into a multi-deposit district-scale system"

And that is no exaggeration, given that the last results lead me to think that Diablillos could turn into a mineral district with multiple deposits, extending towards Laderas, Fantasma and Sombra. That scale potential explains why the next step, the DFS in charge of Worley Ltd., will be decisive.

With an optimized engineering, less capital costs (~C$125M less) and operative efficiencies (~C$15M per year), the project could reach a profitability matched by few.

Catalysts and Risks

The path towards production becomes increasingly clear. For starters, we have to mention that the environmental approval (EIA) is foreseen for Q4 2025, a key step that will mark the start of a construction process.

In parallel, the adhesion to the RIGI regime could be confirmed during Q1 2026, which would grant fiscal stability to ABBRF and would change for 30 years, reducing the corporate tax to 25% and eliminating the export duties. In Argentina, that is not a detail but a structural advantage that very few projects have.

In the meantime, the DFS advances with a sustained rhythm and will be posted throughout H1 2026. It will be the document that defines the final engineering and profitability parameters, before FID, foreseen towards the end of next year.

On the ground, the story is still alive, given that phase V of drilling, with 20,000 meters in course, is still expanding the zones of Oculto East and JAC. To that is added the national electric connection, a logistical breakthrough that will reduce CAPEX a ~C$60M and will improve the long-term operative efficiency.

Each of these steps not only gets closer to AbraSilver in the development stage but also reinforces something more important, which is the perception that Diablillos already belongs to a different league within the universe of silver and gold projects in Latin America.

Of course, the Argentine context imposes challenges. The macroeconomic stability, the FX evolution, and the mining logistics from the North of the country are variables that could alter the times or the costs of the project. Even then, AbraSilver faces those risks in a solid cash position, support of strategic associates and a presence in Salta, the most advanced province of the country in terms of mining institutionality.

That is why I believe that the risk-benefit balance still remains clearly inclined towards the positive side: a solid project, carried out with discipline, in a context that, even with volatility, offers an opportunity window to those who see beyond the short term.

Conclusion

I have closely followed dozens of junior companies in the region, but few show the balance between scale, profitability, and execution that ABBRF has.

It's not an unreachable promise, but a company that executes, that clearly communicates, and that makes breakthroughs over solid basic techniques.

As a world-class project with institutional associates, experimental equipment, and organized finances, AbraSilver has all the elements to become a relevant producer.

In my opinion, ABBRF still remains a buy for investors that seek direct exposure to the silver and gold cycle, backed by tangible fundamentals. And beyond the numbers, there is something that remains unchanged: Diablillos inspires confidence. And in mining, confidence isn't drilled - it's built.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Danaos: A Lot Of Value Even With The Current Scenario
data.description:
On May 29th of this year, I wrote a report on Danaos (NYSE:DAC), in which I explored why the company was a good opportunity even in the face of increased market volatility resulting from the proposed changes to import tariffs that the U.S. government intends to implement. In that report, "Danaos Corp: Another Big Bet on Maritime Transport", I attempted to show the company's advantages not only from a financial perspective but also due to the gap between supply and demand for vessels.

In this report, I sought to update the financial data based on the 2Q2025 reports. At the same time, I intend to recalculate the key ratios mentioned in the previous report. I also intend to update the vessel analysis, focusing on the dry bulk segment, which the company has begun to enter since 2024, taking into account that the outlook for the other segment has not changed significantly compared to what was previously discussed.

Thesis Update

I see fit to begin this update of the thesis I outlined in May of this year by discussing the company's growth in terms of the number of vessels and cargo capacity. As I mentioned previously, Danaos launched its business model to enter the dry bulk vessel segment with the addition of seven vessels in 2023. Danaos acquired another three in 2024, and this is the number they have maintained to date. Regarding the container vessel segment, which is the company's strong suit, after reducing the fleet until 2023, a period of vessel acquisition began. Danaos currently has ten dry bulk vessels and 74 container vessels, representing a capacity of 605.7 TEUs. As we will see later, this fleet increase is interesting, even under a strong deleveraging program by Danaos' board of directors.

It's interesting to note that at the time of this report, the company has a total of $955 million in closed contracts, including both segments, for 2025 and approximately $879 million for 2026. If we consider the total sales volume under confirmed contracts from Q2 2025 onward, the company reports a value of $4.051 billion. However, if we only consider the next two years, through 2027, the value rises to $2.512 billion. This means that Danaos has projected revenue from confirmed contracts equivalent to 160% of the company's value.

Finding these values ​​is, from my point of view, extremely attractive. Especially when we consider the company's ability to renew expiring contracts and maintain vessel utilization capacity above 95%. Therefore, it's expected that these values ​​will remain stable or increase as time goes on and expiring contracts are renewed. Still, knowing that the company's sales value for the next two years is higher than its market capitalization is truly interesting.

I want to analyze a point that I didn't explore in depth in the previous report, but which I believe is worthwhile considering a segment of the company that cannot be ignored. The dry bulk vessel segment is strongly influenced by the evolution of international trade, especially in China. Since the Asian giant is one of the world's leading commodity buyers, it's logical to focus on China. One point the company reports that I find interesting is being able to see how the country will stand in 2025 in terms of daily steel production. As you can see, 2025 is well below the country's peaks in MnT/day, but it is well above the average minimum since 2012.

Turning to seaborne iron ore imports, although 2025 sees a slight increase compared to previous years, the trend following the end of the pandemic is downward. This, as we will see later, has had a negative impact on the daily charter price, especially in the dry bulk segment. As can be seen in the following graph, the greatest impact is generated by the fall of China in particular.

Now, this would seem to be a detriment to the company. However, as we'll see in the next section, Danaos' reported EBITDA in Q2 2025 compared to the same quarter last year has remained virtually stable. This is partly due to the fleet increase we mentioned at the beginning, which offset the price drop. In this context, it's worth asking how the company is doing. If we analyze the key profitability indicators, we see that Danaos continues to show impressive results.

To put this into perspective, we're talking about a company with an EBITDA margin of over 60% and an ROE of over 13%, as can be seen below. If we add to this the deleveraging process, which I'll discuss below, and the company's multiples, the scenario becomes more understandable. I'd like to point out that it's true that the dry bulk vessel market has suffered setbacks due to the slowdown in China's growth. However, the theory regarding the potential gap between new vessel production in the container segment and future global demand remains as valid as in the previous report. As I mentioned previously, Danaos focuses on the smaller vessel segment, which has a much smaller production footprint, while the main shipbuilding companies focus on the larger segment.

If we put all these pieces together, we realize that Danaos is a true Deep Value company. Considering that it continues to maintain profitability and that the results shown have held up year over year, as we'll see below, despite market volatility, I consider it a STRONG BUY.

2Q2025 Results

In this segment, as is customary in my reports, I would like to briefly summarize the company's key financial and economic results reported during the second quarter of 2025. At the same time, I sought to compare with prior periods to broaden the scope of the analysis and examine the evolution of results over the past year.

To begin, I'd like to mention the company's revenue and, with respect to that total, how much corresponds to each of its business lines. In 2Q2025, Danaos reported revenue of $262.15 million. Of that total, $239.45 million came from the container vessel segment, and the remaining $22.7 million came from the dry bulk vessel segment. This provides an idea of ​​the weighting of each business in the company's total revenue. Compared to the same quarter last year, we see a 6.4% increase compared to the $246.31 million in 2Q2024. Compared to the first half of the year, the company has reached a total of $515.46 million by 2025.

Regarding Adjusted EBTIDA, the year-over-year figure remained virtually stable. While Danaos reported $176.04 million in 2Q2025, the figure for 2Q2024 was $176.79 million. This occurred even in a context where the time charter equivalent $/day for container vessels was $36,087 million, while in the second quarter of 2024 it was $37,802 million. Part of this was due to a fleet increase from an average of 69 vessels in 2024 to 74 this year. Looking at the first half of the year, we see that Adjusted EBITDA was $347.71 million. Interestingly, if we annualize this value and compare it to the company's current EV, we see that Danaos' EV/EBITDA ratio is around 2.44x. In fact, if we compare it to the value I reported for the previous report, we see a decrease from the 2.61x I calculated in May.

Another point I'd like to examine is the evolution of Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities. The value reported by Danaos in the second quarter of 2025 totaled $162.78 million. If we compare it with the same period in 2024, we see that in the second quarter of last year the value was $154.27 million. This represents a year-over-year growth of approximately 5.5%. If we look at the value reported in the first half of 2025, what we can see is that the reported value was $296.64 million. If we annualize that value and compare it to the company's current market cap, we can see that the P/CF is around 2.66x. Again, a company that can generate the equivalent of the company's entire market cap in cash from its own business operations in less than three years is extremely attractive.

Another key point I mentioned in the previous report, and one I want to draw attention to again, is the deleveraging process the company has undertaken. As we can see, Danaos went from a net debt-to-Adjusted EBITDA ratio of 7.4x to a ratio of 0.3x.

If we add to this the fact that the company's largest debt amortization payments are expected between 2028 and 2029, from my perspective, the company's solidity and solvency are evident. In this sense, a company with the ratios we have been describing, with an extremely manageable leverage level, with good future prospects, and, as we will see later, better positioned relative to some indicators compared to its main competitors. All of this strengthens the STRONG BUY rating I assigned to the company.

Valuation

In this segment, and unlike what I proposed as a company valuation in the previous report, in which I developed a DCF model, I propose arriving at a target price based on a valuation multiple. To carry out the analysis, it is important to keep in mind that when choosing the companies that would serve as comparables, I selected three direct competitors in the container shipping business: NMM, GSL, and CMRE. I also looked for a comparable in the dry bulk segment: SBLK. The difference in the number of representatives for each segment is linked to the difference in the proportion of the fleet that each segment represents for Danaos' business.

For the analysis, I decided to use four of the ratios I considered most representative of the industry, as well as a series of financial and economic indicators that allow me to show, relative to the selected competitors, Danaos' position in the industry. Regarding the ratios I used for the analysis, I decided to consider PE, EV/EBITDA, P/CF, and finally, P/BV. I did not include the latter, but I decided to make an exception in this case since, for these types of companies, the book value is practically determined by the market price of the vessels, which means there are no distortions in the style of intangibles or brands.

Regarding the analysis of relative industry positioning, I used the EBITDA Margin, the EBITDA CAGR over the last three years, and finally the net debt-to-EBITDA ratio to measure each company's leverage level. Before describing the target price analysis with each of the selected ratios, I would like to briefly describe this point. Finally, regarding net debt-to-EBITDA, Danaos is by far the most financially solvent and least leveraged company. If we analyze the metrics with respect to SBLK, the drybulk company, we see that Danaos is in much better shape. I believe the mixed model allows for risk diversification.

Risks

Before concluding, I would like to mention the potential risks the company may face in the short and medium term. In this regard, the main one I see is the escalation of international trade conflicts. With Trump's return to the White House, the American government established a series of reforms related to the import tariffs that the United States will impose on other countries.

This becomes even more relevant when we consider that one of the main victims of the new US tariff measure is China. Considering that, as I mentioned previously, the Asian giant is one of the world's main consumers of raw materials, a trade conflict between the two largest economies on the planet could lead to a drop in traded volumes. The possibility that tariff changes could lead to a trade war is not a scenario that can be ruled out either.

Conclusion

Despite the risks described above, I believe Danaos has a solid capital structure. The deleveraging process could allow it to weather a period of greater stress. On the other hand, the number of contracts concluded over the next two years is undoubtedly a safety net in the face of potential risks. It is true that there have been periods of volatility in foreign trade, but the company has demonstrated its ability to maintain fleet occupancy rates at very high percentages and its ability to sustain the results generated even in these scenarios. If we add to this the ratios and the advantage compared to other companies in the industry, as well as the upside potential reflected in the valuation multiples, I consider it an excellent option.
